clean_story:
Tiny particles. Unpredictable rules.

The name quantum physics comes from quantum—the smallest possible packet of energy or matter, like a single drop of water in an ocean you can’t see. Classical physics says objects move smoothly, like a ball rolling downhill. Quantum physics says they jump between fixed states, like a light switch flicking on or off with no in-between.

Here’s the strange part: Particles don’t have definite positions until you measure them. An electron in an atom isn’t a dot but a fuzzy cloud of probabilities, spread out like dice yet to land. Shine light on it, and the cloud collapses—suddenly, the electron picks a spot. This isn’t guesswork; equations predict these odds with terrifying precision. GPS relies on it: Satellites adjust for quantum shifts in atomic clocks, or your location would drift kilometers off.

Even weirder: Particles can be entangled. Measure one, and its twin instantaneously "chooses" a matching state, no matter the distance. Einstein called this "spooky action," but experiments confirm it—information travels faster than light’s speed limit, without breaking relativity. Why? No one knows. The math just works.

Three related subjects are wave-particle duality, quantum computing basics, and how cats explain superposition.
